title: Abernathy Pass via Cedar Creek
author: BeckyS
report_text: Hiked to the waterfall and back (4 miles). We were the only car in the lot at 7:20 am and saw only one family of hikers on our way back. Some parts of the trail a little overgrown and trees over the trail in four places, but easy to get through. Still quite a bit of water in Cedar Creek and the falls and a beautiful display of Fireweed. This fire-ravaged area (2021) is now hosting a plentiful array of grasses, wildflowers, shrubs and young trees.

title: Abernathy Pass via Cedar Creek
author: Rolan
snow: Intermittent snow - not hard to cross
report_text:
It's definitely shoulder season. The fall colors are mostly faded or gone. The snow has started, but not enough for snowshoes or skis.

I hadn't been up Cedar Creek beyond the falls since the 2021 fire. I wanted to see how the forest was recovering. Even with the snow cover, I noticed a few pine seedlings along the trail. Most of the post-fire vegetation is brown as a harbinger of the winter to come.

There is 1/2" of snow at the falls and 4" of snow at the meadow area where I turned around.

There has been a lot of work restoring the trail and clearing the logs since the fire. The trail tread is in good condition.

The campsite below the meadow at 4 miles is covered with a few inches of snow.

Unfortunately, the low brush covered by recent snow meant I was soaked below the knees by the time I turned around.

Still, it was a great trip!

title: Abernathy Pass via Cedar Creek
author: Rolan
trail_conditions: Obstacles on trail:Trees down across trail.
report_text:
As Abam noted on May 31, the trail to the falls is in good condition with flowers blooming. There is a large log with a few other logs at 0.6 mile that has a short, steep boot path around.

I was able to go in 6.2 miles to the stream 0.2 miles before the West Fork Cedar Creek crossing.

From 1.7 to 4.1 miles, the trail is in the recovering forest from the 2021 Cedar Creek Fire. There are lots of fireweed, low brush, and some seedlings. The trail has been logged out through this section.

From 4.1 miles to 5.1 miles, the trail is a pleasant walk mostly through an old growth forest with some open areas.

Beyond 5.1 miles, the trail is back in a recovery forest from the 2006 Cedar Creek Fire.

The are 26 logs over the trail from 4.2 miles to 6.2 miles. Hikers will have some difficulty with the logs at 4.9 miles.

Most of the large secondary streams are flowing. Crossing these is pretty easy except for the ones at 5.2 miles and 6.2 miles which must be forded or crossed on sketchy logs upstream or downstream of the ford.

The camps at 2.6, 3.8, 4.3, and 4.4 miles are all usable.

There were very fresh tracks over hikers' tracks by a moose heading up the trail, from 0.9 to 2.6 miles.

Mosquitoes were somewhat bothersome in the camp at 3.8 miles and beyond, when stopped.

title: Abernathy Pass via Cedar Creek
author: Matthew Rieke
snow: Trail snow-covered at times - gear and expertise recommended
report_text: Hiked a total of 16 miles out and back. Trail is in good condition with a handful of blow downs but none difficult to navigate over/under. A couple of stream crossings require ability to travel on fallen logs elevated above stream or full height waterproof boots. Intermittent snow begins at 4.5 miles but isn't too difficult to cross with some postholing. Persistent snow and loss of trail begins around 7 miles. No bugs after departing the trailhead.

title: Blue Lake,Heather - Maple Pass Loop,Abernathy Pass via Cedar Creek,Driveway Butte,Cutthroat Lake&Thunder Knob
author: Austineats
snow: Snowfields to cross - could be difficult
report_text:
Full disclosure we did not hike any of these trails but thought some planning help might be good for folks who are wondering what conditions might be like.

We drove highway 20 today, Friday and checked out conditions along the way. Let me start with the most popular hikes.

Blue Lake has parking for a dozen vehicles at most and about three feet of snow on the ground. There will be continuous snow the entire way.

Heather/Maple Pass has zero parking. There is a huge berm of snow blocking parking, go somewhere else🙁

Cutthroat Lake has snow from just about the turn off from hwy 20 so add one mile of walking to this trip. Driveway Butte is clear to the parking and then hikable for the first few miles. Expect snow once you’re in the burn area.

Cedar Creek’s parking is readily accessed and is probably snow free for quite a ways.

Many of the more westerly highway 20 hikes should be easily accessed, Falls Creek Falls, Thunder Knob, Happy Creek, Gorge Creek Overlook….

The Methow Valley is gorgeous, most trails are open and the wildflowers are just bonkers.

Be safe, and enjoy your day.

title: Abernathy Pass via Cedar Creek
author: Austineats
report_text:
Despite the massive burn over of this area in 2021, this trail is in great condition. Today we headed out late in the day just to see how conditions were and to look for morels. No luck on mushrooms but the wildflowers were great.

The wildfire burned heavily here and up Sandy Butte to the south and east. It hopscotched a bunch burning some trees to the ground and leaving others untouched. Two years later, first succession plants like arnica are just covering everything😁

No trees down in the trail, tread in perfect shape, we only went a bit further than the falls. Maybe you’ll have time to get to Abernathy Pass.

title: Abernathy Pass via Cedar Creek
author: Chris
trail_conditions: Obstacles on trail:Trees down across trail,Overgrown in places.
report_text:
We hiked the burned over Cedar Creek trail to about 4600.  The trail has been logged out to about 1/2-mile past Cedar Falls.  Beyond that, you'll find plenty of logs to climb over and the trail is hard to follow in places, i.e., the trail is a mess in places. We could not find the trail through a heavily burned and blown down section about 4 miles from the road.  That took us a long time until we finally found the old trail next to the creek that had not burned.  Eventually we connected back up with the current tail. Thankfully after that, we got into lightly burned areas and finally areas that had not burned.  We had some wonderful views up the trail looking through Aspens glowing in the sunshine.  The portions of this trail beyond Cedar Falls get little use and hence, little maintenance.  You need to pay attention to stay on the trail even in the meadow portions.

We left the trail a bit southwest of the creek that flows from the tarn at 7141. Close to the trail, the route up through the woods has considerable blowdown, even though it did not burn. We ascended on SW of the creek to about 5000 - 5200 feet, then on the NE side to the tarn. It was a bit windy, so we camped a little below the tarn at 6800. From the tarn, we climbed the green parts of the talus field above and on climbers right of the tarn. We then made an ascending left traverse below first cliff band and then up to the obvious, wide, reddish gully that goes to the SE ridge. Below the ridge (maybe 15 metres), we followed an ascending ramp right for 100 metres and then followed the POLR (path of least resistance) to the ridge near the false summit. This portion is mostly class 1 and 2 with an occasional class 3 step.  We passed below the false summit on the south side and then ascended to the ridge. The route then descends on class 4/low class 5 rock for a short distance (we used a rope for about 8 metres). Then it follows a ledge with an overhang to a gully that is an easy scramble a short distance to the summit.

Silver Moon is a very enjoyable, classic North Cascades climb!

title: Abernathy Pass via Cedar Creek
author: Ranger Jim
snow: Trail snow-covered at times - gear and expertise recommended
report_text: Snowshoed in about two miles, gorgeous deep snow through the burn of last summer, big views over the Methow Valley and of Goat Rocks.

title: Abernathy Pass via Cedar Creek,Cutthroat Pass via Cutthroat Lake,Mudhole Lake,Wolf Creek,Buck Mountain Snowshoe,Tiffany Mountain via Freezeout Ridge,Tiffany Lake,North Twentymile Peak,Billy Goat&Burch Mountain via Billy Goat Pass
author: Abam
trail_conditions: Obstacles on trail:Trees down across trail.
report_text:
Although both fires (Cedar Creek and Club Creek 2) have been somewhat stabilized (~25% contained), summer heat will return and the drought has not abated, just increased.  In addition, a whole series of new fires have emerged (from August 2 until mid-day August 6, the WA State DNR had responded to 107 incidents of reports of smoke or flame.  Most were small, but so far six have grown to over 500 acres.

Rain and higher humidity associated with the thunderstorms of August 3 - 4 suppressed both fires and enabled some catch-up and mop up work to be completed.  However on August 4th, air temperature in Mazama reached 95 and humidity 15% while at Washington Pass these were 76 and 21%.  Today, August 5, temperature and humidity at Mazama reached similar values.  These conditions plus the forecast for strong winds coming from the NW and NNW have the potential to drastically increase the fire danger from the two present fires as well as awakening some of the lightning strikes quiet until now.  Some 21 other strikes have already resulted in immediate responses.

As a result of two fires in the Methow Valley (Cedar Creek Fire and the Cub Creek 2 Fire) and the associated smoke, air pollution, and closures, for all practical purposes these valleys are closed and day hikes and overnights are both impossible and would be dangerous if undertaken.  This report is a combination of two former reports (Cedar Creek Fire: https://www.wta.org/go-hiking/trip-reports/trip_report-2021-07-10-1565507752 and Cub Creek 2 Fire: https://www.wta.org/go-hiking/trip-reports/trip_report-2021-07-17-3031663437 ).

Fires now totaled over 110,000 acres burned and will have dramatic and visual impacts on the area.  In addition, these two fires represent a continuation of major fires that since 1990 have probably impacted over 60% of the entire Methow River Watershed (1,890 square miles or 1,209,600 acres).  That is a lot of fires in 30 years.  Fires were an integral part of this landscape for thousands of years; set frequently by both natural and anthropogenic sources (i.e., indigenous people).  With the death of indigenous people (between 1500 and 1850 between 80 and 90% of the original population died), their placement on reservations, their death or imprisonment when they were caught setting fires, active fire suppression, and mis-management of forest lands including high-grading, clearcutting, and over grazing, forest structure and condition were set for disease, insects, and fire.  Wednesday's op-ed piece in the Seattle Times (August 4) by Hilary Franz, Phil Rigdon, and Mike Stevens entitled "How we can restore forests, increase fire resilience, and protect communities" is an important read!

Excellent Web Page for Information: https://methownet.com/firereport.html

title: Abernathy Pass via Cedar Creek
author: CarolW
trail_conditions: Obstacles on trail:Trees down across trail,Overgrown in places.
snow: Trail snow-covered at times - gear and expertise recommended
report_text: Started @8:30, 65*. Trail recently opened after last summer's fire closure. Trail is clear for about 2 miles.  About 1/2 mile beyond the falls( beautiful and running full!) The fire damage really start to appear.  Washouts, brush and LOTS of dead fall.  A few mosquitoes. We turned around just shy of the 3 mile mark. A good friend tried it last week and hit snow at the 5 mile mark.  Definitely wear pants and boots. I'd be wary of the trail in high winds, at this time. Plenty of standing burned out and dead trees. Burned root holes as well. Ran into  USFS trail repair crew on our way out. (Thanked them profusely for all the hard work)

title: Abernathy Pass via Cedar Creek
author: Trip Report latestarter
trail_conditions: Obstacles on trail:Trees down across trail,Mud/Rock slide or washout.
snow: Intermittent snow - not hard to cross
report_text: A sunny, grand day to be on the trail! About .4 miles from the trailhead, there is a minor washout; easy to step over. There was a Methow Valley Trails Collaborative (MVTC) crew of 2 cleaning up storm damage with chainsaws. Stopped and chatted with them for a bit. I think there were 40+ logs between the MVTC group and the falls. Patchy snow up to the falls and deeper, more consistent snow beyond them. I turned around about a quarter-mile past the falls because of snow. Sat by the effusive falls a while before continuing back to the car. Yellow Avalanche Lily was beautiful in the midday sun! I took out my saw, a Silky Bigboy 2000, a handful of times to limb and cut smaller logs. I bring a saw to do my part to maintain trails on public lands and I encourage you and other hikers to take one as well. After flush cuts out of the trail corridor, my path to the car continued. I found the MVTC workers clearing a particularly scratchy, limby mess as I rounded a bend. I had to saw a path through this mess on my way up and it was still difficult to get past, so I was grateful for the clear path. Also the site of another easy to cross washout. I talked with the sawyers for a while and gave them my thanks for keeping trails clear for all of us! They said ~70 logs had been cleared since yesterday, Monday 4/6, along both the road to trailhead and the track to the falls. They are working their way toward the falls and beyond; I'm excited to see how far they clear. Total hike = 4.4 miles, 708' gained
